petitioner_background: The petitioner sought to classify the beneficiary as an employment-based immigrant of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. The beneficiary was listed on Form I-140 as an executive director of clinical research, responsible for leading clinical research operations from protocol development to final research results. The director characterized the beneficiary's field as an 'executive and manager' in the health care business, specifically connected to developing an organizational model in southern Maryland that the petitioner claimed could be replicated elsewhere.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner failed to submit any evidence regarding the awards criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director indicated that the petitioner failed to submit any evidence regarding the awards criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(i). Not contested on appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence claimed to demonstrate qualifying memberships, but specifics were not described in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director thoroughly analyzed the documentary evidence and determined that the petitioner failed to establish that the beneficiary met the membership criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(ii). On appeal, counsel made no reference or additional arguments regarding the membership criterion, so the AAO considered this issue abandoned, citing Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen. and Hristov v. Roark.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence claimed to demonstrate published material about the beneficiary, but specifics were not described in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined that the petitioner failed to establish that the beneficiary met the published material criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii). On appeal, counsel made no reference or additional arguments regarding this criterion, so the AAO considered the issue abandoned.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of the beneficiary serving as a judge of the work of others.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined that the petitioner established that the beneficiary met the judging criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iv). This was the only criterion found satisfied.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted expert letters, including a letter from an expert who described the beneficiary's development of an organizational model in southern Maryland. The letter stated: 'What makes [the beneficiary's] accomplishment so unique has been his ability to not only expand his model, but also to provide an organizational structure that others might use in Maryland and across the country. His model makes perfect sense for many states and local communities to use as a map to show how to create such a model. [The beneficiary's] contributions to the model is one in which others could follow his lead in building an [organization] where ever it might be needed.' The letter also stated the beneficiary's 'work in Maryland may help lead the way for others to replicate his work throughout the United States.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director specifically referred to and quoted from the expert's letter on page 8 of the decision, contrary to counsel's claim that it was disregarded. The AAO concurred with the director's assessment that the letters did not explain how the beneficiary's role as an executive director impacted the field as a whole but only his employer and southern Maryland. The letters did not demonstrate how the beneficiary's concepts were being implemented by other organizations throughout the nation or internationally. The expert used tentative language ('might or could,' 'may help lead the way'), indicating future rather than present impact. A petition cannot be approved based on expectation of future eligibility; eligibility must be established at the time of filing under 8 C.F.R. §§ 103.2(b)(1), (12) and Matter of Katigbak. The AAO emphasized that letters solicited in support of an immigration petition are of less weight than preexisting, independent evidence of original contributions of major significance, citing Matter of Caron International and Matter of V-K-. The phrase 'major significance' has meaning per Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund. The petitioner failed to meet 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(v).

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence claimed to constitute scholarly articles, but specifics were not described in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined that the petitioner failed to establish that the beneficiary met the scholarly articles criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vi). On appeal, counsel made no reference or additional arguments regarding this criterion, so the AAO considered the issue abandoned.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence claiming the beneficiary played a leading and/or critical role at an organization (name redacted).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director thoroughly and effectively analyzed the claims and determined that the petitioner did not submit independent information about the organization and failed to demonstrate that it has a distinguished reputation. The petitioner also failed to submit official documentation from the appropriate authority establishing that the beneficiary performed in a leading or critical role. Counsel offered no argument demonstrating error by the director, simply restating the director's finding. Additionally, the AAO noted that section 203(b)(1)(A)(i) requires extensive evidence and the plain language of 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(viii) uses the plural 'organizations or establishments,' requiring a leading or critical role in more than one organization with a distinguished reputation. The AAO contrasted this with criteria at 8 C.F.R. §§ 204.5(h)(3)(iv) and (ix), which are worded in the singular, and cited Maramjaya v. USCIS and Snapnames.com Inc. v. Chertoff as supporting the significance of plural versus singular in regulations.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence claimed to demonstrate a high salary, but specifics were not described in the AAO decision.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director determined that the petitioner failed to establish that the beneficiary met the high salary criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(ix). On appeal, counsel made no reference or additional arguments regarding this criterion, so the AAO considered the issue abandoned.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner failed to submit any evidence regarding the commercial successes criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director indicated that the petitioner failed to submit any evidence regarding the commercial successes criterion pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(x). Not contested on appeal.

outcome_reasoning: The appeal was dismissed because the petitioner satisfied only one of the ten regulatory categories (judging) and thus failed to meet the antecedent regulatory requirement of three types of evidence under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Counsel's argument that the director had improperly narrowed the beneficiary's field to 'researcher' was rejected as unsupported, since the director had consistently referred to the beneficiary as an 'executive and manager' and never discounted evidence on that basis. The AAO also deemed the membership, published material, scholarly articles, and high salary criteria abandoned because counsel failed to address them on appeal. Although the AAO stated that the evidence was not indicative of a level of expertise consistent with the small percentage at the very top of the field or sustained national or international acclaim, it declined to conduct a full final merits determination under Kazarian because the petitioner had already failed the antecedent three-types-of-evidence requirement. The burden of proof under section 291 of the Act was not sustained.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm'r 1988)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
Silverman v. Eastrich Multiple Investor Fund, L.P., 51 F.3d 28 (3rd Cir. 1995)
Maramjaya v. USCIS, Civ. Act. No. 06-2158 (RCL) (D.C. Cir. March 26, 2008)
Snapnames.com Inc. v. Chertoff, 2006 WL 3491005 (D. Or. Nov. 30, 2006)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Several procedural issues: (1) Counsel indicated on Form I-290B that a brief and/or additional evidence would be submitted within 30 days, but over four months later nothing further had been received, so the record was considered complete as it stood. (2) Counsel requested oral argument in his cover letter but provided no explanation of necessity; USCIS denied the request because counsel identified no unique factors or issues of law, and the written record fully represented the facts and issues. (3) The AAO found several criteria (membership, published material, scholarly articles, high salary) abandoned because counsel failed to raise any argument regarding them on appeal. (4) Counsel's assertions on appeal that the director narrowed the field or disregarded an expert letter were found to be unsupported by the record and not entitled to evidentiary weight per INS v. Phinpathya. No fraud or misrepresentation findings.
